comparator.noListSelected: Select at least one list to start comparing.
comparator.positioningSectionDesc: Visualization of the relative positioning of lists on key municipal issues.
comparator.metaDescription: Compare the programs of candidates for the 2026 Montpellier municipal elections.

positioning.title: 2026 Ideological Compass
positioning.description: Visualize how lists position themselves on key issues for the Montpellier municipal election.
positioning.microText: Axes run from conservative approaches (left) to progressive approaches (right).

valuePages.noMeasures: No measure matches this positioning in our comparator.
valuePages.excludedNote: Reactionary candidacies fall outside the republican spectrum. This comparator does not include any candidate with this positioning.
valuePages.safetyArticleLabel: Read our analysis on the effectiveness of security policies

economy.1: Priority given to market mechanisms, free competition, and deregulation. Municipal action is limited to facilitating private investment. Public services play a secondary role relative to private initiative.
economy.2: The market remains the reference framework, but safety nets, redistribution, and regulation correct its excesses. Municipal action complements the market without structurally challenging it.
economy.3: Social justice and ecological transition are inseparable. Critique of productivism and growth at all costs. Priority given to local and solidarity economies, circular economy, and inequality reduction in the transition.
economy.4: Municipal public ownership, commons, cooperatives, and local economic democracy. The city becomes a lever for transformation toward greater equity and collective self-determination, challenging private ownership of essential services.

societal.1: Return to a prior social order, restriction of civil liberties and minority rights. This positioning is outside the republican spectrum and is not represented in this comparator.
societal.2: Attachment to traditional social structures and authority. Distrust of rapid social change. Social order is seen as the primary guarantor of cohesion.
societal.3: Support for individual rights, diversity, and LGBTQ+ equality within the existing economic framework. Anti-discrimination policies without challenging the liberal model.
societal.4: Structural transformation of social hierarchies. Intersectional approach articulating feminism, anti-racism, and social justice. Critique of systemic forms of domination.
societal.5: Extension of moral consideration to all sentient beings beyond the human. Critique of speciesism as a form of domination. Promotion of animal rights and veganism as a political choice.

governance.1: Decisions concentrated in the hands of the executive. Limited citizen consultation. Technocratic or charismatic leadership style. Efficiency comes through hierarchical command.
governance.2: Classic liberal democracy: citizens elect representatives who govern on their behalf. Participation is mainly limited to voting.
governance.3: Democracy is exercised between elections. Citizens' assemblies, participatory budgets, deliberative processes. Residents are consulted and involved in specific decisions.
governance.4: Radical democratic vision. Self-managed commons, direct democracy, neighborhood councils with binding power. Challenging the representative model in favor of permanent citizen governance.

security.1: Security through deterrence, repression, and visible force. Cameras, zero tolerance, reinforced municipal police. Punitive approach to antisocial behavior.
security.2: Classic law enforcement, reactive rather than proactive. Police respond to reports. Neither strongly punitive nor strongly preventive.
security.3: Community-based policing rooted in the territory. Agents known to residents. De-escalation and mediation as priority tools. Building trust between institutions and communities.
security.4: Security is rooted in social prevention. Addresses root causes of crime: inequality, school dropout, addiction. Community initiatives alongside — or instead of — police. Inspired by evidence-based criminology.

ecology.1: Environmental protection compatible with continued economic growth. Clean technologies, green infrastructure, carbon markets. The growth paradigm is not challenged.
ecology.2: Acceleration of energy and ecological transition while maintaining economic dynamism. Strong investments in renewable energy, public transport, and thermal renovation — within the existing economic model.
ecology.3: Challenging the productivist economic model. Binding ecological planning with quantified objectives. Assumed incompatibility between unlimited growth and environmental preservation.
ecology.4: Voluntary and organized reduction of production and consumption in wealthy societies. Post-growth economy centered on well-being, sobriety, and planetary limits.
